claim: An agent is an LLM-powered application that uses tools to achieve goals, not just a chatbot; agents can run asynchronously for data processing, mental health support, finance management, and other non-chat use cases.
actionable: When building agents, consider non-chat use cases and aynchronous execution rather than defaulting to chatbot interfaces.
source_title: Building agents with Amazon Bedrock AgentCore | Serverless Office Hours

claim: MCP (Model Context Protocol) is a standardized interface for agents to access tools and resources, similar to REST but designed for agent consumption of APIs and databases.
actionable: Adopt MCP as a standard interface when building agent tools to ensure agents can discover and understand tool capabilities through schema.
source_title: Building agents with Amazon Bedrock AgentCore | Serverless Office Hours

claim: Most MCP servers are not actually servers but local processes downloaded and run on machines; downloading arbitrary MCP servers poses security risks equivalent to running untrusted code.
actionable: Never download and run untrusted MCP servers locally; instead, deploy MCP servers in sandboxed remote environments or verify source legitimacy before local execution.
source_title: Building agents with Amazon Bedrock AgentCore | Serverless Office Hours

claim: Lambda functions have 15-minute timeouts, making them unsuitable for stateful MCP server connections that need to maintain session state across multiple invocations.
actionable: For stateful MCP servers with session requirements, use container-based deployments or serverless platforms that support longer execution times rather than Lambda.
source_title: Building agents with Amazon Bedrock AgentCore | Serverless Office Hours

claim: Bedrock AgentCore is a platform for deploying agents and MCP servers that supports long-running sessions (up to 8 hours) while only charging for execution time, solving the Lambda timeout and ECS always-on cost problems.
actionable: Use Bedrock AgentCore to deploy stateful agents and MCP servers without worrying about 15-minute Lambda limits or paying for idle ECS container time.
source_title: Building agents with Amazon Bedrock AgentCore | Serverless Office Hours

claim: AgentCore Memory stores conversation histories and summaries across sessions, enabling multi-session agents and agent-to-agent knowledge sharing without implementing custom state management.
actionable: Use AgentCore Memory to persist conversation state across long-running sessions instead of building custom persistence layers.
source_title: Building agents with Amazon Bedrock AgentCore | Serverless Office Hours

claim: AgentCore Gateway exposes Lambda functions and existing APIs as MCP servers without requiring code changes, enabling legacy systems to work with agents through a standardized interface.
actionable: Deploy AgentCore Gateway in front of existing Lambda functions or APIs to expose them as MCP servers without application code modifications.
source_title: Building agents with Amazon Bedrock AgentCore | Serverless Office Hours

claim: Agents don't have inherent workflow control; workflow orchestration and state management happen inside the agent framework (LangChain, Strands, LlamaIndex, etc.), not in AgentCore.
actionable: Choose an agent framework that provides workflow orchestration primitives rather than expecting AgentCore to manage complex multi-step workflows.
source_title: Building agents with Amazon Bedrock AgentCore | Serverless Office Hours

claim: MCP tool schemas and descriptions are essential for agents to understand available capabilities; agents use schemas to match task requirements with available tools dynamically.
actionable: Always provide complete MCP tool schemas and clear descriptions so agents can discover and correctly apply tools based on task context.
source_title: Building agents with Amazon Bedrock AgentCore | Serverless Office Hours

claim: You can use any LLM with agents deployed on AgentCore or accessing MCP servers; the LLM choice is decoupled from deployment infrastructure.
actionable: Choose LLMs based on capability and cost rather than deployment constraints; use OpenAI, Anthropic Claude, or open-source models with agents deployed on any platform.
source_title: Building agents with Amazon Bedrock AgentCore | Serverless Office Hours

claim: AgentCore Gateway supports tool namespacing to prevent name conflicts when multiple MCP servers expose tools with the same names.
actionable: Use tool namespacing in AgentCore Gateway when deploying multiple MCP servers to avoid tool name collisions.
source_title: Building agents with Amazon Bedrock AgentCore | Serverless Office Hours

claim: Code Interpreter is a built-in AgentCore tool that provides a sandboxed environment for agents to run code without risking the agent process itself.
actionable: Use AgentCore Code Interpreter when agents need to execute dynamic code rather than embedding code execution directly in agent processes.
source_title: Building agents with Amazon Bedrock AgentCore | Serverless Office Hours

claim: Browser Use is a built-in AgentCore tool enabling agents to browse the web and interact with web applications without external tool wrapping.
actionable: Leverage AgentCore's Browser Use tool for agents that need web interaction instead of building custom browser automation.
source_title: Building agents with Amazon Bedrock AgentCore | Serverless Office Hours

claim: AgentCore provides IAM-based authentication (SigV4) for agents to connect to AWS resources, reducing the need for separate credential management.
actionable: Use AgentCore's IAM integration for secure agent access to AWS resources instead of embedding credentials in agent code.
source_title: Building agents with Amazon Bedrock AgentCore | Serverless Office Hours
